issues.body: `set_reviewed_sha` still performs an unconditional whole-body PATCH derived from an earlier GET. If a maintainer or automation edits the body after that GET and before the PATCH, the edit is overwritten; the following GET equals `target`, so the function reports a successful bind and cannot detect the loss. The new case 5 explicitly demonstrates this unsafe behavior by asserting that the concurrent edit is lost. Documenting and pinning the limitation is honest, but it does not resolve the prior blocker or provide a fail-closed reviewed-SHA update.
issues.fix_hint: Move the binding to immutable commit-specific metadata or another store/API operation with a real conditional/CAS primitive, so a stale writer cannot replace intervening state. Add a non-vacuous test that injects an edit between the initial read and write and asserts that the edit survives or that the bind fails without overwriting it; the test must not treat silent data loss as the expected passing outcome.
issues.title: Read-to-PATCH race still silently clobbers concurrent body edits

verdict.summary: The prior blocker remains unchanged. The patch now accurately documents and tests the read-to-PATCH race, and it improves behavior for idempotent and post-PATCH cases, but it deliberately retains the trust-plane failure: a maintainer edit between the initial GET and the whole-body PATCH is silently overwritten, after which verification sees the attacker's own target and reports success. A regression test that asserts the edit is lost does not establish the required safety property. Because reviewed-SHA binding still mutates a shared mutable PR body without a conditional write, the change is not sound or complete and must fail the gate.
